nodes.text: You've been staring at those boards for twenty minutes. Either you're trying to memorize them or you're hoping they'll start making sense on their own. They won't.
nodes.subtext: Her posture is precise, practiced. Ex-military or ex-corporate. But her eyes keep drifting to the departure boards like she's calculating routes she'll never fly.

responses.text: I'm trying to learn. Any advice?
Can you help me understand them?
You're reading them too. And you already know the best routes. What would it take to get you to share?

nodes.text: Fair enough. But those boards won't explain themselves, and neither will the margins. If you change your mind, I'll be at this table.

responses.text: Actually... what do you mean by margins?

nodes.text: Elena Reeves. Navigator, currently between contracts. Sit down. I'll walk you through it.

See food listed at 80 here? Breakstone's a mining colony, workers burning four thousand calories a day underground. Food sells for 140 there on a quiet day. That's your margin. Buy here, sell there. Do it enough times, you can afford better cargo.

Watch the event feeds. A mining boom means ore prices drop, buy cheap, sell at stations that process it. A crop failure means food prices spike at the affected system. The boards tell you what's happening. Your job is to be where the demand is before everyone else.

responses.text: You seem to know a lot. What's your story?

nodes.text: I'm between contracts. Let's leave it at that.

responses.text: Come on, there's got to be more to it.
Fair enough. Thanks for the trading lesson.

nodes.text: I used to navigate for a Guild shipping company. Route planning, fuel optimization, the boring stuff that keeps convoys on schedule. I left. Reasons are my own.

responses.text: I understand. Thanks for the help, Elena.

nodes.text: Good luck out there, Captain. The boards get easier once you stop trying to read them and start trying to read the people buying from them.
So you're the one running cargo out of Nexus. I read your docking manifest. Light cargo, light weapons, light on experience. Let's see what you're made of.

responses.text: How do things work at Breakstone?

nodes.text: Hanna Voss. Dock boss. I decide who trades here and who doesn't. The Guild sends traders through with paperwork and permits, we don't care about paperwork.

responses.text: Then how do I earn trading rights?

nodes.text: We don't hand out permits to strangers. You want to trade at Breakstone? Prove you're not another Guild vulture looking to squeeze us. Bring five units of food to the miners' commissary. Feed my people. Then we'll talk about your trading rights.

responses.text: Five units of food. I can do that.
Tell me about those murals on the walls.

nodes.text: Because my miners eat in shifts. Four thousand calories a day, hauling rock in pressure suits. We grow nothing here. Every meal comes from off-station. So when a trader shows up, the first question is, are you here to help or to take?

The founding strike. Seventy years ago, Breakstone miners walked off the job because a Guild contractor cut their air recycler maintenance budget. Three people died before the Union organized. Those murals make sure we don't forget.
Good. Buy it at Nexus Prime or wherever you like. Bring it back here. I'm not charging you and I'm not filing paperwork. Just show me you're not wasting my time. Get moving.
What did you say your name was?
nodes.subtext: His hands stopped moving when he heard your voice. Whatever he's thinking, he's working hard to keep it off his face.

responses.text: I didn't. Why do you ask?

nodes.text: You related to someone who worked the Nexus Prime orbital? Mining crew, maybe eight, nine years back?

Your father worked a contract rotation through Breakstone before transferring to the Nexus orbital. He filed a complaint about the air recycler units on the station. Complaint came to my engineering team. I flagged it. Wrote a report. Recommended immediate inspection.
Nothing. Got filed. Got buried. Six months later your father's dead and three other workers are in medical with lung damage. I've been carrying that for years.
Doesn't matter whose fault it is. What matters is nobody fixed it. The system decided it was cheaper to replace workers than repair equipment. That's the math they do.
Because I was twenty-four and I thought filing a report meant something. I know better now. You file, they file it. You escalate, they reassign you. That's how it works.
Yeah. I don't have a good answer either.
Look, you're pulling contracts at a mining station and you don't know how to mine. That's going to get you killed or broke. Probably both. Let me show you the basics.
Because I couldn't help your father. And I don't want to see another green pilot get themselves killed on dead rock because nobody taught them to read a vein.

Rule one: your suit is your life. Check your seals twice before you go out. Rule two: see that discoloration on the rock? Dark streaks are iron sulfide, worthless. Follow the vein left and you'll hit magnetite. That's money. Rule three: don't get greedy. Fill your hold, get back to station, sell.

responses.text: Got it. Seals, veins, don't get greedy.

nodes.text: The system doesn't care about people like your father. Doesn't care about people like me, either. We file reports, we flag problems, and the people with the money decide it's cheaper to replace us than fix what's broken. You've got your own ship. That's more than most people out here ever get. Don't waste it.

Excuse me. Dr. Priya Osei, Research Director, Materials Analysis Division, Axiom Research Institute. I require transport to Axiom Labs. My chartered vessel has been reassigned, citing pirate risk. These research samples are time-sensitive. You appear to have a functioning ship and a current bill of landing.

responses.text: I can take you there. What's the pay?

nodes.text: Mineral composition samples from three separate extraction sites. The isotope ratios degrade within seventy-two hours of collection. If I cannot deliver them to the laboratory, six months of fieldwork becomes... statistically insignificant.

responses.text: I'll take you. How much are you offering?

nodes.text: I... understand. If you reconsider, I will be here. For approximately another four hours, after which the samples become unusable.

Thank you. I can offer 200 credits upon arrival. The Collective values fair compensation for services rendered. Shall we depart? I should mention. I may have questions during the transit. Your lived experience constitutes a primary data source that peer-reviewed literature consistently underrepresents.

I study resource extraction economics. The relationship between infrastructure investment and worker mortality in orbital mining operations. Your father worked a mining orbital near Nexus Prime, yes? The air recycler failures there were, statistically speaking, a predictable outcome of inadequate infrastructure investment.

responses.text: He wasn't a statistic. He was my father.
What about you? Why study mining economics?

nodes.text: I, yes. Of course. I apologize. That was... imprecise. I meant the system failed him. Not that he was. I am sorry. The Collective values precision, sometimes at the cost of... other things. I did not intend offense.

responses.text: It's okay. I know you meant well.
Just... be more careful with your words.

nodes.text: The Collective has published extensive research on orbital safety standards. The failure rate at facilities like the one your father worked at is forty-seven times the published acceptable rate. My paper aims to demonstrate that the economic model incentivizes neglect. Fascinating from a systems perspective, devastating from a human one.

I grew up on Axiom Labs. I have never visited a mining station. I have studied these systems for years from the inside of a laboratory. I am beginning to suspect that I did not understand what I was studying. I think... I think that matters.

We have arrived. Thank you, Captain. Your compensation, as promised. I want you to know. Dr. Okafor, who founded this institute, said 'Knowledge that does not heal is knowledge wasted.' I hope my research can live up to that. I hope you will return. I would like to continue our conversation.

responses.text: I might take you up on that.
Good luck with the research, Dr. Osei.

nodes.text: Well, well. A new face at Haven's Rest. Sit down, eat something, you look like you've been living on ration bars. There's flatbread and stew. Alliance rules: you feed strangers first, ask questions later.

Tomas. Most folks call me Drifter. I scout the frontier routes, mapping safe passages, charting jump corridors, finding the shortcuts nobody else bothers with. Way I see it, the stars don't care about your schedule. You learn to work with them, not against them.

responses.text: You mentioned you're a scout. Good money in that?

nodes.text: Built from salvaged freighter hulls. No two corridors look alike. Farmers sell produce at the Harvest Market, kids run through the corridors, people barter face-to-face. Handshakes close deals here. First place where people knew my name and it wasn't a problem.

responses.text: Sounds like you've been running for a while.
Nice place. So what do you need?

nodes.text: Here's the thing. The Guild raised tariffs on Alliance goods, again. My textiles would sell for 200 a bale at Stellaris Port, but after the tariff, I'd clear maybe 30 credits. That's not trade. That's charity. And I don't take charity.

You've got a bill of landing. Guild rate on the tariff. Take my textiles to Stellaris Port, sell them under your license. We split the profits. Fair deal, and a farmer on Verdant gets to feed her kids this month.

It's... creative interpretation. You have a license to trade. I have goods to sell. The Guild writes rules to protect the Guild. I don't feel real obligated to help them do that. A closed door's just an invitation to find a window.

responses.text: I appreciate the honesty, but no.

nodes.text: You shouldn't. Not yet. Trust is earned, not given. But I'm offering you a fair split on a deal that hurts nobody and helps both of us. That's the best pitch I've got.

responses.text: I need to think about it. Not right now.

nodes.text: You won't. But if you did? Your bill of landing is legitimate. You're selling textiles at Stellaris Port. That's what traders do. Nobody's going to audit a small-time captain moving five bales.
Good. Take the textiles to Stellaris Port and sell them. Your cut will be waiting when it's done. Nobody gets hurt. The Guild still gets their tariff, just less of it. And a farmer gets to eat.
Fair enough. You've got your own line. I respect that. Most people out here draw theirs in different places, doesn't make yours wrong. If you ever change your mind, you know where to find me.

You are the child of an assistant foreman for an asteroid mining operation in the orbit of Nexus Prime. You grew up in an impoverished community, bled dry by corporate greed and wage slavery. As a child, you watched your father pull 16 hour shifts consistently, working himself into an early grave.

Between the stress and the poor air conditioning systems in your colony ship, your father passed away six months ago, at the age of 43. You had only just celebrated your 16th birthday. You have nothing. No path forward, no schooling to fall back on, no family left, and no reason to think tomorrow will be different from today.

You consider staying aboard the colony ship, taking on some menial position. But without anyone to take you in, you'd merely be sinking into the same wage slavery that killed your father. At night, as you toss and turn weighing your options, it torments you to know that nothing gets better. Nothing will improve.

Perhaps there is another path. With the last of your father's savings, you spend a few days cleaning up his possessions before pawning them off at the ship's trading post. You can barely afford the hull of a broken shuttle. A neighbor in maintenance helps you source an engine. As your credits dry out, you only just barely manage to get the shuttle working and charge the fuel rods.

Departing from the colony ship, you find yourself in the orbit of Nexus Prime, with the system at your disposal. You have 4,000 credits to your name. You are woefully unaware of the system, its politics, its people. Your goal is simple, to survive and find new horizons. Anywhere away from here.
I need your bill of landing, and your registration, sir.

responses.text: Here is my registration, but I don't have a bill of landing.

nodes.text: Then I'm afraid we'll be confiscating any goods in your hull, as you are not cleared to import goods at this time.

responses.text: The only items in the hull are personal living supplies.

nodes.text: You'll be able to reclaim those once you pay the 250 credit fee to acquire your bill of landing.
Filing takes about an hour. You can wait in your shuttle or wander the station, but don't try to trade until the stamp clears. Guild auditors love catching that.
There you are. Bill of landing, stamped and registered. You're cleared to buy and sell at any Guild-affiliated port in the Expanse. Try not to lose it. Replacements cost double.
One more thing. I had a look at your shuttle while we processed the paperwork. That thing has no weapons mounted, not even a basic pulse cannon. You've got a weapon slot, but it's empty.

It will be if you wander into the wrong part of space. The routes around Nexus Prime are safe enough, but systems marked as dangerous? Pirates, raiders, they don't ask questions. Without a weapon, you can't fight back. You'd be a sitting target.

Stick to the safe trade lanes for now. Nexus Prime, Forgeworks, places like that. Build up some credits trading, and visit the Shipyard when you can afford to outfit your shuttle properly. A basic weapon makes all the difference when trouble finds you.
Good luck out there, Captain. Space doesn't care how young you are, it'll chew you up just the same. Fly smart.
*A heavyset man flips through shipping manifests, muttering numbers under his breath. He holds up one finger without looking up.* One second. Three shipments behind today.

responses.text: I'm trying to figure out how work moves through here.
What routes pay best right now?
I heard you have a delivery job.
Any word on the ore shipment?
The iron ore's been delivered to Forgeworks.

nodes.text: *Odom sets the manifest down. Looks at you properly for the first time.* Fine. Quick. Any port's board gets its listings from brokers. People like me. We see what moves. We track what pays. You want to know who to ask, it's the people standing at terminals, not the ones staring at the board.

*He drums his fingers on the terminal.* Concord pays for coolant. Always. Union pays for grain, always. Fringe pays for weapons, never legally. You're green. Stick to Concord. Don't go Fringe. Don't ask about Union until you have papers. That's free. Next time, bring credits or cargo.
You there. I'll give you 500 credits to ship this iron ore off to Forgeworks. I just bought it, and I've been told that my carrier is full up. I need it there within the week.

responses.text: Tell me more about the job.

nodes.text: Ten units of iron ore to Forgeworks. My contact there is a clerk named Wren. Find him at the Neutral Ground when you land. Tell him Odom sent you and he'll handle the rest. I already had the ore put in your hold. Didn't figure you'd say no to easy money.
Still waiting on confirmation from Forgeworks. You do still have my ore, right? Ten units, don't go selling it off on me. I've got a buyer on the other end counting on this.
Forgeworks pinged me. All ten units, weighed and accepted. Credits are in your account already. You fly straight and you don't skim. I'll remember that.

responses.text: You know where to find me.

nodes.text: *Odom stops mid-count. His eyes drift to the cargo manifest on his terminal, then back to you. Something cold settles behind them.* Your hold's empty. I can see it right here on the docking log. Ten units of iron ore, consigned under my name, and your bay is reading zero.

You sold it. You actually sold my freight. Consigned cargo, with my name on the manifest, and you walked it over to the trading floor like it was yours to move. For what? Fifteen credits a unit? Twenty on a lucky day? I was paying you six hundred for that run. Six hundred credits, a clean entry on your shipping record, and a name that brokers actually return calls to. You traded all of that for scrap money.

No. We're done talking. I've moved freight through this station for nineteen years, and I have never had a pilot sell consigned cargo out from under me. You are the first. Congratulations. I'm logging a freight violation against your permit, and every broker and dock clerk on Nexus Prime is going to hear about this by tomorrow. You just burned the easiest reputation you were ever going to build, and you did it for grocery money. Now get out of my face before I call station security.
*A wiry man with a datapad balanced on one knee glances up from a stack of cargo manifests.* You here for a pickup or a drop?

responses.text: Odom sent me. Ten units of iron ore.

nodes.text: *He glances up from his datapad.* Oh, the pilot who ran Odom's iron. That shipment cleared fine, by the way. He was pleased. You ever need cargo work through Forgeworks, the board's right there. We always need reliable haulers.

*He checks the manifest, taps twice, and nods.* Odom's shipment. Ten iron, standard grade. Looks right. I'll flag it as received and let him know it made it in one piece. Credits should hit your account within the hour.
Clean run, pilot. He'll remember the favor. Odom was furious when he found out his regular hauler was full. Reputations are built and broken on reliability out here. Remember that.

*A woman in a convoy captain's jacket leans against the viewport railing, fingers drumming the scuffed metal. She watches a Guild freighter line up for docking, eyes tracking it the way a hunter watches the treeline.*

responses.text: Captain Sato? From the distress signal?
Captain. Any news on the convoy raids?
Sato. I've been digging into those convoy raids.

nodes.text: You answered my distress call when nobody else would. Three Guild escorts passed me by, their cargo was more valuable than my crew, apparently. But you stopped. That means something out here.

responses.text: Anyone would have done the same.

nodes.text: I sent a distress signal three days ago. My convoy was hit, raiders disabled my engines and scattered the escort. Your ship was in range. You kept flying.

responses.text: I didn't think I could help.

nodes.text: That's the third convoy hit this quarter on the Nova corridor. Used to be one, maybe two raids a year. Now it's every other week. And the raiders, they knew exactly where we'd be. Timing, heading, cargo manifest. Someone is feeding them information.

responses.text: That sounds like more than random piracy.
[Persuasion 2] Any idea who's behind the leaks?

nodes.text: I'm not here to guilt you. I'm here because my convoy was the third hit this quarter. Used to be rare, now it's every other week. The raiders knew our route, our timing, our cargo. That's not luck. That's intelligence.

responses.text: Why are you telling me this?

nodes.text: I filed reports with the Guild. They said they'd investigate. That was six weeks ago. Nothing. I've been running escort for twelve years and I've never seen the Guild drag their feet on piracy. Something doesn't add up.

responses.text: What are you going to do about it?

nodes.text: You're sharp. I pulled the patrol rotation schedules before my last run. The gaps in coverage, they're not random. Someone is adjusting the patrol windows right before each attack. I've got the timestamps saved. Take them. Maybe they'll mean something to the right person.

responses.text: Who should I show this to?

nodes.text: I'm going to keep running convoys. Someone has to. But if you hear anything, from the bars, the docks, the places I can't go in a Guild uniform. I want to know. The people on those freighters are counting on someone to figure this out.

responses.text: I'll keep my ears open, Captain.

nodes.text: Nothing new. Filed another report with the Guild, same dead end as the last three. Another convoy hit last week on the Breakstone corridor. Same pattern. They know when the patrols thin out. I keep running the routes because someone has to, but whoever's pulling the strings hasn't slowed down.

responses.text: I'm still asking around. I'll find something.

nodes.text: I can see it in your face, you've learned something. I won't ask who you've been talking to. The less I know about your sources, the less I have to put in a Guild report. Just... be careful. Whatever this is, it's bigger than convoy raids.

responses.text: I'll watch my back. You do the same.

nodes.text: Easy, easy. Don't look around like that, you'll draw attention. Sit down. Buy a drink. Look bored. That's the first rule of Nexus Prime cantinas.

responses.text: Why are you talking to me?

nodes.text: Dex Halloran. I sell information. Buy it, too, when the price is right. I know every trader, smuggler, and customs officer between here and Crimson Reach. And I know you've been asking questions about missing convoys.

responses.text: How do you know what I've been asking about?

nodes.text: Because you helped Reva Sato, and that puts you on a very short list of people in this system who actually give a damn. I'm Dex. I deal in information. And right now, I've got a problem that requires someone who isn't me.

See that table in the corner? Union dockworker and a Guild freight auditor, sharing a pitcher like old friends. Six months ago they would have spit in each other's drinks. Something's changed. Money is moving through channels I can't trace, and people who should be enemies are suddenly cooperating.

responses.text: What does that have to do with the convoy raids?
[Persuasion 3] What do you think is really going on?

nodes.text: I think someone is bankrolling the raids. Not pirates, pirates don't have this kind of coordination. Someone with real resources and real reach. I intercepted a data chip last week from a courier who didn't know what he was carrying. Encrypted, military-grade. I can't crack it, but I know someone at Crimson Reach who might.

responses.text: You want me to deliver the chip.
Why can't you take it yourself?

nodes.text: Maybe everything. Maybe nothing. I intercepted a data chip last week, encrypted, heavy-duty. I need someone to run it to Crimson Reach. There's a woman there, Malia Torres, who can crack anything. I can't go myself. I have too many eyes on me right now.

Because I'm watched. Every dock, every gate. I'm useful to a lot of people, which means a lot of people are paying attention to where I go. A nobody captain running cargo? That's invisible. No offense.

responses.text: None taken. What's in it for me?
I don't like being called invisible.

nodes.text: Take the chip to Malia Torres at Crimson Reach. She runs salvage operations out of the lower decks. Tell her Dex sent you. She'll know what to do with it. And Captain, don't try to read it yourself. The encryption has a wipe trigger. Open it wrong and you lose everything.

responses.text: I don't want to get involved.

nodes.text: Smart choice. I'll owe you one, and in this business, a favor from Dex Halloran is worth more than credits. Stay safe out there. The people behind this aren't amateurs.

Your call. But whatever's on that chip, it's connected to the convoy raids, the money, all of it. If you change your mind, you know where to find me. I'm always at this table. Third stool from the left.
You're the one Dex sent? Figured he'd send someone eventually. Come on, not here. Too many ears in the main bay. Follow me.
Malia Torres. I run salvage operations, the official ones, anyway. Down here, I crack open things people don't want cracked. Your chip. Hand it over.

responses.text: How do I know I can trust you?

nodes.text: You don't. But Dex does, and that's why you're here. I've lived on Crimson Reach for fifteen years. I know every bolt and weld on this station. If I wanted to screw you over, I'd have done it before you walked through that door.
Give me a second... military-grade, like he said. But the header, this isn't raider encryption. This is corporate. Commerce Guild standard, Series-7 cipher. Someone inside the Guild encrypted this.

responses.text: The Guild is behind the raids?

nodes.text: Parts of it. Shipping routes, patrol schedules, convoy manifests, everything a raider would need to hit a target clean. And payment records. Someone with serious resources is funding this. The amounts are... this isn't petty piracy. This is organized. Planned. Bankrolled.

responses.text: What do we do with this?

nodes.text: The names are scrubbed. Whoever set this up knew what they were doing. But I can tell you this, the money trail runs through three systems, and it always circles back to the same place. Someone high up. Someone who benefits from chaos on the shipping lanes. That's all I can pull for now.

responses.text: This is bigger than I thought.
Reva Sato's convoys were hit because of this.

nodes.text: Look. Crimson Reach doesn't play by Guild rules. Down here, we take care of our own. If you want to dig into this, I can help. I've got contacts, tools, and access to channels the Guild doesn't know about. But you'll need to earn your way in.
You've earned yourself access to the underground market. Gear, information, work that never shows up on a Guild ledger. Down here, reputation is currency, and yours just cleared its first deposit.

responses.text: Just point me in the right direction.

nodes.text: One more thing. Keep this quiet. The people funding these raids have contacts on every station in the Expanse. If word gets back that someone's pulling the thread, they'll cut it. And everyone holding on.

Welcome to Axiom Labs. The Governing Council has convened an emergency summit on the pirate threat. Representatives from all four factions are here. I've arranged a seat for you as a neutral observer, you've dealt with each faction, and frankly, none of them trust each other enough to accept anyone else.

The Commerce Guild sent Captain Reva Sato and two trade consuls. The Miners Union delegation is led by Hanna Voss, she insisted on coming personally. The Alliance sent Tomas Drifter and another representative who've been arguing in the corridor for twenty minutes. And I'm chairing the session on behalf of the Collective.

The Commerce Guild proposes a Joint Patrol Initiative. Guild-funded escort corvettes on every major trade route. Standardized transponder protocols. Centralized threat reporting through Guild communications infrastructure. We have the ships, the training, and the logistics network. The pirate threat demands a professional military response.

responses.text: That gives the Guild a lot of control.

nodes.text: 'Professional military response.' That's a nice way of saying Guild warships in every system. Last time we let the Guild 'protect' our shipping lanes, they charged us triple tariffs for the privilege. We'll arm our own convoys. The Union doesn't need Guild oversight to defend our workers.

If I may, the Collective proposes a compromise. A sector-wide sensor network. Real-time tracking of all vessel movements through shared telemetry. No single faction controls the data, it feeds into a neutral analysis system here at Axiom Labs. We identify pirate patterns, we predict strikes, we respond faster.

responses.text: Surveillance of every ship in the Expanse?

nodes.text: With respect, Doctor, and I mean that loosely, you're asking every independent pilot in the Expanse to broadcast their position to a Collective database. Half the Alliance makes a living because nobody tracks where we go or what we carry. You want to end piracy? Arm the stations, share intel voluntarily, and stop trying to build systems that watch everyone.

responses.text: Is there any common ground here?

nodes.text: The session has reached an impasse. The Guild won't fund patrols without oversight authority. The Union won't accept oversight. The Collective won't share sensor technology without data governance rights. The Alliance won't participate in any centralized system. The charter on the wall behind me reads 'Together We Build', but today, together seems very far away.

responses.text: Maybe they need to fail before they'll cooperate.

nodes.text: The room goes quiet as you stand. You speak plainly, about the convoy you saw burning, about the escalating attacks, about what happens if the factions let pride override survival. For a moment, the delegates listen. Reva nods slowly. Hanna crosses her arms but doesn't interrupt. It won't change the vote today. But they heard you.

You stand to address the room, but one of the Guild consuls cuts you off. 'This is a session for faction representatives, not freelance pilots.' Priya tries to intercede, but the moment passes. The delegates return to their entrenched positions.
The session winds down. Delegates gather their materials and exchange stiff courtesies. As the room empties, you have a last chance to observe.

Your eye catches it, barely. One of the Guild trade consuls palms a small data chip to someone in the observation gallery. Plain clothes, no faction insignia. They pocket it and slip through a service corridor. The consul rejoins the Guild delegation as if nothing happened. You glance at Priya. She's watching the door they left through. She saw it too.

The summit concludes with formal statements of 'continued dialogue', diplomatic language for failure. Something about the proceedings nags at you, but you can't place it. Priya frowns at something across the room, then shakes her head. 'Did you see...' She trails off. 'Never mind. Probably nothing.'

Thank you for coming. I know this wasn't encouraging. But having someone in the room who isn't aligned with any faction matters more than any of them realize. Whatever comes next, at least one person saw the full picture today.
You again. Sit down, and don't look at me like that. I heard about the ambush. Military-grade hardware on unmarked hulls. That ring any bells?

responses.text: Guild hardware. I saw the markings.
How do you know about that?

nodes.text: I know because it's my business to know. And because I'm not the only one asking questions. The Guild supply ledgers don't add up, hardware is disappearing off the books. Someone inside is diverting it.

I don't have a name yet. But I have a lead. There's a man at Breakstone. Oren Tak, retired miner. Used to run the deep tunnels near Iron Depths before they sealed them. He saw something down there, something the Union covered up. A facility that shouldn't exist.

responses.text: Why would a miner know about Guild weapons?

nodes.text: The tunnels connect to old freight routes, pre-Union infrastructure that predates the current maps. If someone wanted to move contraband without hitting customs checkpoints, those tunnels would be perfect. Oren can tell you more, but he won't talk to just anyone. You'll need to earn his trust. Head to Breakstone and find him in the lower docks.

You shouldn't. But I gave you the Crimson Run lead and it panned out, didn't it? I'm not doing this out of charity. Whoever is arming those pirates is bad for everyone, including my business. Oren Tak, lower docks, Breakstone. Don't keep him waiting; he spooks easy.
You're not Union. I can tell from the way you walk, no dust on your boots, no calluses. What do you want with an old tunneler?

responses.text: Dex Halloran sent me. He said you saw something in the deep tunnels.

nodes.text: Then pass through. I've got nothing to say to tourists.

responses.text: Actually. Dex sent me. About the tunnels near Iron Depths.

nodes.text: Dex. That name buys you thirty seconds. The tunnels are sealed for a reason, and I sealed my mouth about them for a reason too. Union brass made that very clear when they pensioned me off early.

responses.text: [Persuasion] Whatever you saw, it's connected to the pirate attacks. People are dying on those convoy routes.
What did the Union threaten you with?

nodes.text: Threaten? Nothing so dramatic. They just made my pension conditional on my silence. Forty years in the tunnels, and my retirement depends on pretending I never saw what I saw. You think I'm going to throw that away for Dex Halloran?

responses.text: [Persuasion] People are being killed with military weapons that shouldn't exist. Your pension won't mean much if the whole sector falls apart.

nodes.text: Look, I can see you mean well. But I'm not ready to stick my neck out. Not yet. Maybe if things get worse... come find me again. I'm not going anywhere.
Oren stares at the table for a long moment. His jaw works like he's chewing something bitter. Then he leans forward and lowers his voice.

Three years ago, my crew broke through into a side gallery near Iron Depths. Sublevel 7, beyond the mapped sections. There was a facility down there. Not Union-built. The architecture was wrong, too clean, too modern. Reinforced bulkheads, atmospheric scrubbers, cargo lifts that went deeper than anything on the official schematics. And crates. Hundreds of crates, all stamped with Commerce Guild freight codes.

responses.text: Guild crates in a Union mine?

nodes.text: I didn't get to open them, foreman pulled us out within the hour and the tunnel was sealed by morning. But I recognized the freight codes. They were the same format used for military procurement. Weapons, ship components, drive cores. Whatever that facility is, it's a staging point. And someone with both Guild access and Union cooperation built it right under Breakstone.

responses.text: That's the connection. Thank you, Oren.

nodes.text: Don't thank me. Just... be careful who you tell. The people running that facility has friends in high places, maybe in every faction. And if they find out I talked... He trails off and stares into his drink. Iron Depths, Sublevel 7. That's all I know.

A woman in a Guild logistics uniform slides into the seat across from you. Her eyes are bloodshot and her hands are shaking. She glances at the cantina door before speaking. You came out of the sealed sections. I saw you on the security feeds before I wiped them. We need to talk.

Sienna Vek. Systems engineer, Commerce Guild Logistics Division. I was transferred here fourteen months ago to maintain environmental controls for what I was told was a deep-core mining research project. It's not a research project.

responses.text: Why should I trust a Guild engineer?

nodes.text: Because I've been watching shipments come through that facility for over a year and the manifests don't match the cargo. Because three engineers who asked questions were reassigned to stations that don't appear on any Guild directory. And because last week I found schematics on the secure network that made me stop sleeping.

Gravitational lensing arrays. Sixteen of them, each the size of a cargo shuttle. The design specs reference something called Project Convergence. The arrays are designed to focus gravitational force into a single point, an artificial singularity. Not a weapon in any conventional sense. A singularity would collapse everything within its gravitational radius. Ships, stations, asteroids. The star itself.

responses.text: They're building a weapon that could destroy the entire Expanse?
That's impossible. You can't just create a black hole.

nodes.text: That's what I thought. Then I checked the energy calculations. The Aurelia star outputs enough energy to bootstrap the process, you don't create the singularity from nothing, you use the star's own mass against it. The arrays redirect gravitational force inward. Once it starts, it's self-sustaining. The star collapses, and everything in orbit goes with it.

responses.text: Why would the Guild destroy the system they profit from?

nodes.text: That's what I can't figure out. The Guild's entire operation depends on the Expanse. Tariffs, trade routes, mining concessions, all of it gone. Unless they have somewhere else to go. The schematics reference coordinates outside the Expanse. Another system, maybe multiple systems. Whoever authorized Project Convergence isn't planning to be here when it fires.

responses.text: [Persuasion] I need copies of those schematics. Everything you have on Project Convergence.
Who authorized this? Who in Guild leadership signed off?

nodes.text: The authorization codes trace to the Guild's Inner Ledger, the executive council that handles off-book operations. No individual names on the documents I've seen, just Ledger seals. But the logistics chain runs through Nexus Prime. That's where the coordination happens.

responses.text: [Persuasion] Give me the data. Schematics, authorization codes, everything. I'll make sure the right people see it.
Come with me. You're not safe here.

nodes.text: I can't. If I disappear, they'll know someone talked. Right now I'm just another engineer running atmosphere scrubbers. I'm more useful here, watching. But I can give you what I've found so far.

responses.text: Then give me everything you have.

nodes.text: She slides a data chip across the table. It's smaller than a thumbnail. Encrypted schematics, energy calculations, shipping manifests, authorization seals. Everything I could pull without tripping the access logs. Project Convergence. Sixteen gravitational lensing arrays. An artificial singularity pointed at the heart of the Aurelia Expanse. Someone has to stop this.

She hesitates, fingers tightening around the data chip in her pocket. I want to trust you. But if this gets intercepted, I'm dead. The people running this project don't leave witnesses. She pauses. I'll tell you what I know. But the raw data stays with me until I'm sure you can actually do something with it.

Project Convergence. Sixteen gravitational lensing arrays being assembled in sections across the Expanse. The Guild's Inner Ledger authorized it. The arrays are designed to collapse the Aurelia star into an artificial singularity. I don't know the timeline, but the manufacturing pace has accelerated in the last two months. Whatever they're planning, it's soon.

responses.text: That's enough. I know what to look for now.

nodes.text: Be careful. The Ledger has people in every faction, that's how they built this facility on Union territory without anyone asking questions. Don't assume anyone is clean. She stands, straightens her uniform, and walks out of the cantina without looking back.

Priya is waiting in her lab when you arrive. The holographic displays are dark except for one, cycling through structural diagrams you recognize from the facility. She looks like she hasn't slept. I've been running the numbers since you sent the preliminary data. Tell me you brought more.

responses.text: I found a Guild engineer. She confirmed everything.

nodes.text: She slots the chip into her terminal. Data cascades across every display in the lab. Her hands move through holographic schematics, pulling apart the array designs layer by layer. After several minutes of silence, she sits down heavily. It's real. The energy calculations are elegant, actually, whoever designed this understood stellar mechanics better than anyone in the Collective. Sixteen gravitational lensing arrays, positioned in a Dyson configuration around the Aurelia star. When activated, they redirect the star's own gravitational output inward. The collapse is self-sustaining within minutes.

responses.text: How long until they can activate it?
Can we warn the other factions?

nodes.text: Based on the manufacturing data from Iron Depths, they've been building components for at least eighteen months. The shipping manifests show accelerated delivery schedules over the last two months. If the assembly site is operational... weeks. Maybe less. We need to act now.

responses.text: Can we take this to the Governing Council?

nodes.text: I already tried. The moment I flagged an emergency session, the Guild trade consul invoked Article 7, a security veto. They blocked the session before I could present a single data point. When I went directly to the Union delegation, Hanna Voss refused to meet. The facility was on Union territory. She's terrified of being implicated.

Malia Torres listened. She believes us. But the Alliance doesn't have the ships or the political weight to move against the Guild unilaterally. And without the Union or the Collective backing them officially, any Alliance action would be treated as piracy. The Guild has spent decades building exactly this kind of institutional paralysis.

responses.text: Then the official channels are useless.

nodes.text: Priya stares at the holographic star model rotating above her desk. Every faction has a reason not to act. The Guild controls the narrative. The Union is compromised. The Collective won't move without consensus. And the Alliance is too small. She looks up at you. That leaves us. If we can find where they're assembling the arrays, we might be able to disable them before activation.

responses.text: The shipping manifests might have coordinates.
I know someone who can find it.

nodes.text: The manifests reference a relay point, cargo is shipped to Iron Depths, processed, then forwarded to coordinates listed only as 'Fulcrum Station.' The name doesn't appear in any public registry. But the coordinate format is consistent with deep-space installations, beyond the standard trade lanes. Someone with the right contacts might be able to triangulate the exact position.

responses.text: Dex Halloran. He found the Iron Depths lead. He can find this.

nodes.text: Your information broker? At this point, I'll take help from anyone who isn't actively trying to collapse a star. Go. Find the assembly site. I'll keep working on the data, there might be a way to disable the arrays remotely if we can access the control systems. And... be careful. If the Ledger knows we have this data, they'll come for all of us.

Dex is already at your usual table when you walk into the cantina. He's not smiling. I heard what happened at Axiom Labs. The Guild blocked the emergency session before your scientist could open her mouth. That's not standard procedure, that's a cover-up at the highest level.

responses.text: I need to find Fulcrum Station.
How did you hear about Axiom Labs already?

nodes.text: I have ears in every station in the Expanse. It's what keeps me alive. And right now, those ears are telling me that Guild security teams are being redeployed from trade lane patrols to an undisclosed location. Whatever you found at Iron Depths has them spooked. They're accelerating.

responses.text: Then we need to find Fulcrum Station. Now.

nodes.text: He pulls out a navigation chart and unfolds it across the table. I've been cross-referencing the shipping manifests from Iron Depths with drive signature data from my contacts along the freight lanes. Every shipment that leaves Iron Depths with Fulcrum routing passes through a gravity shadow near the outer ring, a region where sensor coverage is minimal.

He taps a point on the chart, deep in the outer ring, far from any established trade route. The Fulcrum. A decommissioned refueling platform from the early colonial period. Officially, it was scrapped thirty years ago. Unofficially, someone rebuilt it into a full orbital assembly facility. That's where they're putting the arrays together. And based on the traffic volume I'm seeing, they're nearly done.

responses.text: Can I get there without being detected?

nodes.text: Guild security corvettes on rotation. At least four, maybe more. Automated defense platforms around the perimeter. And the station itself will have internal security, the same kind of hardware those unmarked raiders were carrying. This isn't a smuggler's den. It's a military installation disguised as a scrapyard.

responses.text: Any way to thin out the patrols?

nodes.text: He folds the chart and slides it across to you. There's a maintenance corridor on the station's lower ring, original colonial construction, never updated. If you can get past the perimeter patrols, that's your way in. I've loaded the coordinates and the approach vector onto this chip. He pauses. This is the end of the road for me. I deal in information, not suicide missions. Whatever happens at the Fulcrum, you're on your own.

responses.text: You've done enough. Thank you, Dex.
If I don't come back, make sure people know what happened.

nodes.text: He stands and straightens his jacket. For what it's worth. I hope you stop them. The Expanse isn't perfect, but it's home. He walks out of the cantina without looking back. On the table, the navigation chip blinks with coordinates to a station that doesn't officially exist.

Every alarm on the ship fires at once. Elena's voice cuts through the noise, sharp with controlled terror. The arrays are moving into position. All sixteen. I'm reading gravitational distortions across every sensor band, the star is already responding. Captain, this is happening now.

responses.text: Get us to the warp gate.

nodes.text: She pulls up the nav display. The Aurelia star pulses wrong on the hologram, a rhythmic contraction that makes your eyes ache. Minutes. The arrays are creating a feedback loop with the star's own gravity. Once it passes the cascade threshold, nothing stops it. Nothing.

responses.text: Then we use the gate. Now.

nodes.text: Marcus is already at the engineering console, rerouting every spare watt to the engines. The gate's powered but not calibrated. If we jump blind, we could come out anywhere, inside an asteroid, in the corona of a star, in empty space a thousand light-years from anything. He looks up. But we stay here, we die for certain.

responses.text: We jump blind if we have to. Spin up the gate.

nodes.text: Marcus shakes his head. Not from here. We'd need someone who understands the Convergence schematics, the same math that collapses the star could calculate a stable exit point. And we'd need friends on the other side of that gate.

responses.text: Priya, you studied the Convergence data. Can you calibrate the jump?
Spin up the gate. We're out of time.

nodes.text: Priya is already moving, pulling up the Convergence schematics on her terminal. She speaks without looking up. The array positioning data includes spatial coordinates, reference points for the gravitational field calculations. I can invert the model. Instead of collapsing space, we use it to navigate. Give me ninety seconds. Her fingers blur across the console. Seventy-eight seconds later, she looks up. I have a stable exit point. A star system, outside the Expanse. Atmosphere readings suggest habitable planets. It's not random, it's where the Ledger was planning to go.

responses.text: They were building an escape to a new home.

nodes.text: The warp gate flares to life. Blue light floods the viewport, intensifying until it hurts to look at. Through the cockpit windows, the Aurelia star shudders visibly, a sphere of fire pulling inward on itself, its surface rippling like water in a storm. The arrays are fully deployed now, sixteen points of light arranged in a perfect sphere around the dying star. Elena's voice is barely a whisper. It's beautiful. It's the most terrible thing I've ever seen.

responses.text: Tomas, hail the Alliance. Tell them to follow us through.

nodes.text: Tomas is already on the comm. Malia? It's Tomas. Open channel, broadband. Bring everyone. Static. Then Malia Torres's voice, rough with static and fear. We see it. The whole Frontier fleet is burning hard for your position. Don't you dare close that gate before we're through. On the sensors, a cluster of drive signatures converges on the warp gate. Small ships, civilian hulls, everything the Alliance has.

responses.text: Hold the gate. Everyone comes through.

nodes.text: The ship hits the gate threshold and reality folds. For an instant that lasts forever, everything dissolves. The hull, the crew, the dying star behind you. Just light, and pressure, and the sensation of being pulled apart and reassembled in a place the universe hasn't decided exists yet. Then it's over. Dark. Silent. Stars, but wrong stars. Different constellations, different colors. A yellow sun hangs in the viewport, steady and calm and utterly alien.

Through the rear sensors, the warp gate still glows. And beyond it, through the narrowing aperture, you can see the Aurelia Expanse for the last time. The star is gone. Where it burned for billions of years, there is a point of absolute darkness, a wound in space that swallows light. The stations, the trade lanes, the asteroid fields, Nexus Prime, Breakstone, Axiom Labs, Iron Depths, Crimson Reach, all of it spiraling inward. Hanna Voss. Officer Larsen. Oren Tak. Sienna Vek. Dex. Everyone who stayed. The gate flickers. Then closes.

Nobody speaks for a long time. Marcus is the first. His voice is flat, mechanical, the voice of a man putting one word in front of the other because stopping means falling. Hull integrity nominal. Life support stable. Fuel reserves at forty-two percent. He swallows. We're alive.

Elena pulls up the nav display. It's empty. No mapped systems, no trade routes, no faction boundaries. Just raw sensor data painting an unknown sky. But the yellow star ahead is stable. Three planets in the habitable zone. And the sensors are picking up structures, artificial, orbital, cold. Someone was here before us. Someone built something and left. She turns to you. Captain. We made it. We're somewhere no one in the Expanse has ever been. Her hand trembles on the console. What do we do now?

responses.text: We survive. We explore. And we find out who built this place.
We remember what we lost. And we make sure it meant something.

nodes.text: Elena nods. She enters the first coordinates into the nav computer, the yellow star, the habitable zone, the silent structures waiting in the dark. The engines hum. The ship turns toward an unknown sun. Behind you, the gate is gone. The Aurelia Expanse is gone. Everything you knew is gone. But your crew is here. Your ship is here. And ahead of you, in the light of a star that has no name, something new is beginning.
Captain. A word, if you have a moment. I'm Neve Osei. I deal in information. The kind that doesn't show up on public ledgers.
nodes.subtext: She chose this table for the sightlines. Two exits within reach, back to the wall. Whatever she's selling, she's been burned before.

responses.text: I don't deal with information brokers.

nodes.text: The chip needs to reach Stellaris Port, Captain. Every hour it sits in your cargo hold is an hour someone could intercept it.

I've come into possession of a sealed data chip. Internal Guild audit records. The kind that would interest certain parties at Stellaris Port, people who keep the books honest. I need someone with a ship and no Guild entanglements to deliver it.

responses.text: Why can't you deliver it yourself?
I'll do it. What's the pay?
I'll deliver it. But I want a copy of whatever's on it. Information has value on both ends of a delivery.

nodes.text: Need-to-know basis, Captain. The less you carry in your head, the less anyone can pull out of it if things go wrong. You'll thank me for that later.

responses.text: Fair enough. I'll take the job.
I don't carry things I can't verify.

nodes.text: Because I'm known. My face triggers alerts at three Guild checkpoints between here and Stellaris. You're small-time, no offense, which means nobody's watching your cargo manifests yet. That's an advantage.

responses.text: None taken. I'll deliver your chip.

nodes.text: Good. Deliver the chip to the contact at Stellaris Port. Don't open it, don't scan it, don't talk about it. Payment on confirmation of delivery. And Captain. Director Harlan Vey has been skimming tariff revenue for years. That chip is the proof. Handle it carefully.
Your prerogative. But that chip represents accountability, something in short supply out here. If you reconsider, I'll be around. Information doesn't expire, Captain. People do.
*She checks the corridor behind you before making eye contact.* You fly independent? Not Guild-contracted, not Union-dispatched? I need to be sure.
nodes.subtext: The clipboard she's holding has been rewritten three times. Whatever decision she's making, she's been making it for weeks.

responses.text: That's me. What do you need?
How are we doing on the documents?

nodes.text: Get those documents to the journalist at Nexus Prime. The longer we wait, the more workers get hurt.
Petra Vance. Factory floor supervisor at the Forgeworks smelting plant. Or I was, until I started asking the wrong questions about the ventilation reports.

Workers on the third shift have been getting sick. Headaches, nosebleeds, two people collapsed last month. I pulled the air quality logs and they've been doctored, someone's overwriting the toxin readings before they hit the official reports. Management knows. They're covering it up.

responses.text: Why not take this to the Union?

nodes.text: I pulled the original maintenance logs before they could overwrite them. Medical records too. Thirty-seven workers with respiratory damage, and the dates line up perfectly with the ventilation failures management 'fixed' on paper. A journalist at Nexus Prime has been waiting for something like this. I just need someone to carry it past Forgeworks security.

responses.text: I'll get it there. No one should have to work in those conditions.
This sounds like it could make me enemies at Forgeworks.
I'm sorry, I can't get involved in this.

nodes.text: The Union rep here answers to the plant manager. Everything I file gets 'reviewed' before it goes anywhere. I need this to bypass the local office entirely, straight to the safety board at Breakstone. That's where the real Union lives.

responses.text: Give me the data. I'll make sure it gets there.
I can't risk getting tangled in factory politics.

nodes.text: It might. But those workers are breathing poison every shift, and nobody's doing a thing about it. My father died in a plant like this. Bad air, bad equipment, bad people looking the other way. I won't look the other way.

responses.text: Neither will I. I'll take the data.
I understand, but I have to pass.

nodes.text: Thank you. *She presses a data module into your hand, her grip tight for a moment.* The journalist's name is on the chip. Get to Nexus Prime and put this in their hands. And Captain? Don't dock at Forgeworks on the way out. Security scans everything that leaves.

responses.text: I'll split the data across two ships. My cargo manifest shows routine freight. Even if they scan one, they won't find the full picture.

nodes.text: I understand. You've got your own problems. But if you change your mind... those workers don't have a lot of time. The longer this goes on, the worse it gets.
*An older man with thick hands and tired eyes flags you from a bench near the docking bay.* You make runs to Breakstone? I've got something that needs to get there. It's not cargo. Not exactly.

responses.text: I haul cargo. What's the job?

nodes.text: You still have that package, Captain? Henryk is at Breakstone. He won't wait forever. Neither will I.
My son, Henryk. He left Forgeworks three years ago. We had words, the kind you can't take back. He's at Breakstone now, working the deep shafts. Hasn't answered a single message I've sent.

responses.text: What do you need me to deliver?

nodes.text: The labor disputes. Henryk sided with the workers pushing for a strike. I told him to keep his head down, keep working, don't make waves. He said I was a coward. Maybe he was right. But I was trying to protect him, the only way I knew how.

responses.text: What do you want me to bring him?

nodes.text: His mining lamp. The one he carried as a boy, following me through the maintenance tunnels. He used to clip it to his belt and pretend he was leading expeditions. *He turns the lamp over in his hands, then holds it out.* I just... I want him to know I remember.

responses.text: I'll make sure he gets it.
Have you tried going to Breakstone yourself?
I'm sorry, I'm not heading that way.

nodes.text: I'm seventy-one years old with lungs full of smelter dust. Doctor says I shouldn't even be walking to the market, let alone booking passage off-station. This lamp is all I've got left to send.

responses.text: I'll deliver it. You have my word.
I'm sorry, Tomasz. I can't help.

nodes.text: Thank you. His name is Henryk Brennan. Works the deep shafts at Breakstone. Tell him... tell him his father still has the other lamp. The matching one. He'll understand.
No, that's... that's alright. I'll find another way. Or I won't. Either way.

*A woman intercepts you near the cantina entrance, dark circles under her eyes, hands gripping a crumpled prescription slip.* Please. My daughter Lenna, she needs medicine that comes through Forgeworks. The embargo has the whole supply chain locked down. I've asked everyone on this station. You're the first independent pilot I've seen in weeks.

responses.text: I can help. What do you need?

nodes.text: My daughter's fever hasn't broken. Please, Captain, the medicine.

Lenna's eight. She's got a lung condition, dust exposure, same thing half the kids on this station deal with. There's a treatment compound that keeps it under control, but the supply line runs through Forgeworks. With the embargo, it's been three weeks since our last shipment.

responses.text: I'll get the medicine. Tell me what you need.

nodes.text: Her lungs scar over. Permanently. The doctor says if she goes another two weeks without treatment, the damage won't be reversible. She's eight years old, Captain. Eight.

responses.text: Tell me exactly what I need to get.

nodes.text: Forgeworks locked down outbound shipments three weeks ago. Leverage against the Union's safety demands. Medicine, food supplements, machine parts, all sitting in a warehouse while bureaucrats argue about contract language. Lenna doesn't care about contract language. She just wants to breathe.

responses.text: Smuggling past an embargo is risky.

nodes.text: I know what I'm asking. I know the risk. But I'm a miner, not a pilot. I can't fly out there and get it myself. All I can do is ask someone who can. Please.

responses.text: I'm sorry. I can't take that risk.

nodes.text: Bronchial stabilizer. Five units would last her two months. Forgeworks has it stockpiled, or Axiom Labs manufactures it fresh. Either way, you'll need to bring it to me directly, not through the customs queue. The embargo scanners flag it automatically. *She pauses.* I won't pretend I can pay what this is worth. But I'll give you everything I have.

responses.text: Keep your credits. I'll get the medicine.
I'll take the job. We'll sort payment later.

nodes.text: I... thank you. I don't know what to say. Just bring it back. That's all that matters. Lenna's counting the days. So am I.

responses.text: I'll be back as soon as I can.

nodes.text: Thank you. Hurry, Captain. Please.
I understand. But if you change your mind... she doesn't have long.

*An older woman in a tailored coat catches your arm with a jeweler's grip. Her smile has been practiced in front of mirrors.* Cassiel Maren. I deal in pre-colony antiquities. You look like someone who moves between stations without attracting much attention. I could use that.

responses.text: We need to talk about the appraisal results.

nodes.text: The collection won't appraise itself, Captain. I trust you haven't lost your nerve.

Nothing illegal, I assure you. Merely... delicate. The antiquities trade thrives on reputation, and reputations are fragile things. A misattributed piece, a careless word, fortunes can turn on a whisper.

I've acquired a collection of pre-colony artifacts, ceramics, metalwork, navigational instruments from the founding era. A buyer at Haven's Rest wants an independent appraisal before committing to purchase. I need you to transport the collection and oversee the appraisal. The fee is generous, 500 credits, plus expenses.

responses.text: 500 credits for a delivery and an appraisal? That's good money.
Why not handle the appraisal yourself?
I'll pass. Sounds too clean to be clean.

nodes.text: The buyer prefers a neutral party present during the assessment. Independent verification protects both sides. Standard practice in the trade. I find that transparency builds trust, and trust builds profit.

Excellent. The collection is crated and ready. Deliver it to the Haven's Rest cultural exchange office and attend the appraisal. Report back to me with the results. A pleasure doing business, Captain.

responses.text: I'll handle the delivery. But I want to run my own appraisal on the way. If these pieces are what you say they are, the independent assessment strengthens your buyer's confidence.

nodes.text: A pity. Well, the offer stands. Good art, and good opportunities, are worth a second look.
Captain. You look troubled. I trust the appraisal went... smoothly?

responses.text: The artifacts are forgeries, Cassiel. Every single one.

nodes.text: I... how thorough was the appraiser? Some of those pieces are remarkably well-crafted reproductions, I'll grant you, but to call them all. Captain, please. Let's discuss this calmly.

responses.text: The isotope dating was conclusive. These were made in the last decade, not the founding era. I'm reporting this to the Guild trade authority.
Here's what's going to happen. You're going to pay me double, and I forget what the appraiser told me.
I don't want any part of this. Keep your credits. I'm walking away.

nodes.text: You're making a mistake. The Guild trade authority will bury this in paperwork for years. And I have friends, Captain, influential friends. But if your conscience demands it... I suppose I always knew this day would come.

responses.text: Your friends can't change isotope dating results.

nodes.text: You're sharper than I gave you credit for. Fine. Double the fee. But this stays between us, Captain. Permanently.
Captain, wait. I... thank you. For not making this worse than it already is. I know what I am. I just didn't expect anyone else to care enough to find out.

*A man in a clean jacket approaches from the docking corridor. He's been watching you refuel for ten minutes.* Captain. I need transport to Haven's Rest. I'll pay 200 credits up front and stay out of your way the entire trip. I travel light.

responses.text: 'No questions' usually means there are questions worth asking.
I'm not taking passengers right now.

nodes.text: Still here, Captain. Ready to leave whenever you are.

Family. My cousin runs a supply shop there. I've been meaning to visit for months, but scheduled transports keep getting rerouted. You know how it is, the big carriers don't prioritize the frontier stations.

responses.text: Fair enough. 200 credits and you've got a ride.
Sorry, I'm headed the other direction.

nodes.text: Nothing dramatic, I promise. I'm just a traveler who'd rather not spend three days waiting for a scheduled transport. Your ship is here, I'm here, and 200 credits seems fair for the convenience.
Appreciate it, Captain. I travel light, just the one bag. I'll stay out of your way. Wake me when we arrive.
No worries. I'll find another ride. Safe travels, Captain.

The eastern fields are dying. Whatever this blight is, it's spreading fast, took out forty hectares in the last week alone. I've heard Axiom Labs has a pesticide compound that could save the harvest, but the Collective won't sell it outside their member systems.

responses.text: Why won't the Collective sell to you?
What do you need from me?

nodes.text: The eastern fields are getting worse. If you've found a way to get that pesticide, we need it now.

If we lose the eastern fields, that's a third of Verdant's grain output for the season. Prices spike, people go hungry. And not just us. Breakstone buys sixty percent of their food stock from our harvests. Haven's Rest, the smaller stations, they all depend on what grows here. When Verdant's fields fail, the whole Expanse feels it within a month.

responses.text: What can I do to help?

nodes.text: Restricted exports. The Collective keeps their best research compounds for member systems. Verdant's Alliance territory, we don't meet their 'collaboration standards,' whatever that means. It's bureaucracy, Captain. The kind that lets crops rot while committees debate.

responses.text: So how do we get it?

nodes.text: Way I see it, there are a few paths. You could buy it at a premium from a third-party dealer, expensive, but legal. You could try convincing someone at Axiom Labs to make an exception. Or you could find a way to get it out without their permission. I'm not picky about the method. I'm picky about my fields surviving.

responses.text: I'll figure out a way to get the compound. Count on it.
I'm not sure I can help with this one.

nodes.text: That's all I needed to hear. The compound is called CX-7 blight retardant. Axiom Labs manufactures it. However you get it, bring it back to Verdant. We'll handle the application. And Captain, a good harvest feeds a lot of people. You'd be doing more good than you know.
I understand. Not every fight is yours to take on. But if you hear of anyone who can help, point them my way. The blight doesn't wait for anyone.

I've spent forty years cultivating this strain. It survives in soil that kills everything else, high salinity, heavy metals, conditions that would wither standard crops in a week. If something happens to Verdant, I want it preserved somewhere safe.

responses.text: What exactly are you asking me to do?
Why are you worried about something happening to Verdant?

nodes.text: Have you delivered the seed samples yet? Three stations, Captain. The sooner they're distributed, the safer the strain.

I'm seventy-three years old, Captain. I've watched blight seasons wipe out entire greenhouses. Trade embargoes that cut off seed shipments for months. Any one of those could end a strain permanently. And these hands won't be sorting specimens forever. Someone needs to have copies of this collection before my next bad year turns into the last one.

I've prepared three sealed seed samples. I need them delivered to agricultural contacts at Breakstone, Stellaris Port, and Haven's Rest. Three separate locations, redundancy ensures survival. Each contact has been briefed and has the cultivation protocols. All I need is a captain willing to make the deliveries.

responses.text: I'd be honored to help preserve your life's work.
Three stops is a lot of travel. What's the compensation?
I'm sorry, I can't commit to that many deliveries right now.

nodes.text: I'm a botanist, not a merchant. I can offer what I have, credits enough for fuel, and the knowledge that you've done something that matters. In fifty years, when that wheat feeds people on stations that don't exist yet, your name will be part of that story. The Collective teaches us that knowledge shared is knowledge doubled.

responses.text: That's enough for me. I'll do it.
I'll need to think about it.

nodes.text: Oh, thank you. Thank you, Captain. Here, the samples are sealed and labeled. Handle them gently. Forty years of work fits in the palm of your hand. Isn't that something? Breakstone, Stellaris Port, Haven's Rest. Three deliveries. Three chances for this strain to survive no matter what happens.

responses.text: I'll take good care of them, Doctor.

nodes.text: I understand. Everyone has their own route to fly. If you meet another captain willing to make the trip, I'd be grateful for a referral. The seeds will keep for a while yet. I'm the one running out of time.
My sister was on the transport Meridian Crossing. It left Stellaris Port eight months ago and never arrived here. The last coordinates in the shipping registry point toward Crimson Reach.

responses.text: What do you know about what happened?
What are you asking me to do?

nodes.text: Any news about the Meridian Crossing? I check the boards every morning, but you'd get word before I would.

I know. I know what that means. But the registry shows the Meridian Crossing transmitted a distress signal before it went dark. Someone out there might have heard it. Found wreckage. Found something. I can't keep sitting here imagining what happened. Even the worst answer is better than this.

Almost nothing. The transport company went bankrupt two months after she disappeared. Their records are gone. All I have is her name. Dalia Hadik, the ship's registry number, and the last known coordinates. Nobody's looked for her. Eight months, and nobody's looked.

responses.text: What do you want me to do?

nodes.text: Fly to the last known coordinates near Crimson Reach. Scan for wreckage, distress beacons, anything. If there are salvage crews in the area, ask if anyone found the Meridian Crossing or its passengers. I'm not asking you to fight pirates. Just... look. Please.

responses.text: I'll search the coordinates. If there's anything to find, I'll find it.
Crimson Reach is too dangerous. I'm sorry.

nodes.text: Thank you. Here are the coordinates and the registry number. Her name is Dalia. She's twenty-six. She has a scar on her left hand from a soldering accident when she was twelve. I don't know why I'm telling you that. I just... I need someone to know who they're looking for.

responses.text: I'll bring you answers, Soren. I promise.

nodes.text: I understand. It's a lot to ask. But if you ever find yourself near those coordinates... just keep your scanners open. That's all I ask.

*A scarred woman with captain's pins on her collar drops into the chair opposite you and slides a drink across the table.* I hear you're the type that doesn't scare easy. Good. A crew at The Fulcrum lifted something from my hold last week. I want it back, and I'm willing to pay someone who isn't connected to the usual mess.

responses.text: I don't do jobs for pirates.

nodes.text: You haven't brought back my property yet. The Fulcrum isn't getting any friendlier, so I'd move.

Something that belongs to me. That's all you need to know. Look, these aren't honest traders I'm sending you after. They hit my ship while I was docked, took what they wanted, and left. I'm not asking you to rob anyone. I'm asking you to settle a debt.

responses.text: Not interested in the details? Fine. What's the pay?

nodes.text: There's rules, Captain. Not laws, rules. You don't steal from your own crew. You don't hit civilian transports carrying medicine or food. And you don't take a man's personal effects. Vasek's crew broke that last one. So now I'm breaking their hold security. Through you.
400 credits. Half now, half when you bring it back. And I'll owe you a favor, which, out here in the Reach, is worth more than credits. My crew doesn't forget who helps us.

responses.text: 400 credits and a favor from a pirate captain. I'm in.
I'll pass. Good luck finding someone else.

nodes.text: Good. Vasek's crew operates from a berth at The Fulcrum, docking level three, aft section. The item is in their cargo hold. Get in, grab it, get out. Don't make a scene. I want my property, not a firefight.
Your loss. I'll find someone else. But next time you're in the Reach with your shields down, remember. I offered you a chance to be on my good side.

*A young researcher nearly trips over a cable as she rushes to intercept you.* Captain, I'm glad I caught you. Three weeks ago our long-range array picked up a repeating signal past the charted boundary. It has structure, mathematical sequencing, prime intervals. Nothing in our databases matches it. We need an amplifier deployed closer to the source, and our research vessels aren't rated for that debris field.

responses.text: Why me? Don't you have research vessels?
Sounds dangerous. What's in it for me?

nodes.text: The signal is still broadcasting at 47.3-second intervals. We need that amplifier deployed before it shifts again.

A repeating sequence with prime number intervals. The frequency shifts follow a pattern that's almost, and I cannot stress this enough, almost certainly artificial. We've been studying it for three weeks. The signal is faint at this distance, which is why we need the amplifier deployed closer to the source. The deployment zone is in a debris-heavy region near the system boundary.

We don't know. It could be a pre-colony probe that drifted off course. It could be a natural phenomenon we haven't catalogued yet. Or it could be something we've never encountered before. That's why we need better data. The amplifier will let us resolve the signal clearly enough to analyze its origin. The implications... Captain, this could rewrite everything we know about the Expanse.

responses.text: What are the risks of deploying the amplifier?
I'll do it. This is too important to pass up.

nodes.text: Our research vessels aren't rated for the debris density in the deployment zone. Too much mass, not enough maneuverability. A smaller ship, like yours, can navigate the field safely. The Collective's institutional review board would take six months to approve a dedicated mission. The signal could degrade by then. We need someone who can move now.

responses.text: I'm your pilot. Let's do this.

nodes.text: The deployment zone is littered with debris from old mining operations, rock fragments, derelict equipment, unstable ore deposits. Navigation will require careful piloting. There's also the matter of being at the very edge of charted space. If something goes wrong, help is a long way off. I won't pretend it's a casual flight.

responses.text: I've flown through worse. I'll deploy your amplifier.
That's too risky for my ship. Sorry.

nodes.text: The Collective has authorized a research stipend, 350 credits, plus full repair coverage if your ship takes debris damage during deployment. And if this signal turns out to be what I think it is, your name goes on the discovery paper. You'd be part of history, Captain.

responses.text: I'll need more details about the risks first.

nodes.text: Wonderful! Here are the deployment coordinates and the amplifier unit. It's self-calibrating, just release it at the marked position and it will orient itself toward the signal source. Be careful in the debris field, and keep your comms open. I'll be monitoring from Nova Research. This is going to be remarkable, Captain. I can feel it.

responses.text: Let's find out what's out there.

nodes.text: I understand. The debris field is no place for unnecessary risks. But Captain, if you change your mind, this signal won't wait forever. Some discoveries only knock once.

*A woman in dock security blues sits behind a terminal cluttered with evidence bags and coffee rings. She doesn't look up.* Pull up a crate. Reshad Patel, shipping clerk, found dead in Loading Bay C. Pressure seal failure, supposedly. Except pressure seals don't fail on occupied docks. Station brass wants this closed yesterday. I need someone outside the dock's gossip. You fly in, you fly out. That's why you.
nodes.subtext: The coffee rings overlap in layers. She's been at this desk for days. The evidence bags are organized by a system only she understands.

responses.text: I'd like to review the suspects again.
I'm ready to make an accusation.

nodes.text: Reshad Patel. Shipping clerk, Dock 7. Fifteen years on station, not a single write-up. Found dead inside Loading Bay C three days ago. Pressure seal failed while he was inside alone. Official line is mechanical failure. *She pulls up a maintenance log.* Except that seal passed inspection forty-eight hours prior. And the override wasn't triggered from the bay's internal panel. It came from an external terminal.

responses.text: External terminal. So someone vented the bay remotely.

nodes.text: That's how I read it. Only three external terminals can reach Bay C's environmental systems: the dock supervisor's office, the freight operations console, and the cargo inspection booth. Access logs show three people used those terminals the night Patel died.

Torrin Vey, Commerce Guild auditor. Used the dock supervisor's office. Kess Drum, Union freight handler. Logged into the freight operations console. Jun Solis, Frontier trader. Badged into the cargo inspection booth. All three had reasons to want Patel quiet.

responses.text: I've talked to all three leads. What's your read?
I need more time with this.

nodes.text: Torrin Vey. Commerce Guild sent him to audit Dock 7's manifests after discrepancies turned up in quarterly reports. Patel's records showed someone had been skimming cargo weights for months. If Patel filed that report, Vey's entire division would be under investigation. *She taps the screen.* Vey was logged into the dock supervisor's office that night. Says he was reviewing manifest files and never touched the environmental controls. But the supervisor's terminal is the only one with direct override access to Bay C's pressure seals.

responses.text: I've heard enough. It was Vey.

nodes.text: Kess Drum. Union freight handler. Worked alongside Patel for six years until a misrouted shipment two weeks back cost Drum a quarterly bonus. They had a public argument. Co-workers described it as ugly. *She pulls up another log.* Drum was logged into the freight operations console, but his cargo loader's GPS puts him on the far side of the dock during the incident window. Those loaders log position every thirty seconds. He'd have had to sprint across the dock, override the seal, and sprint back without a gap in the position log.

responses.text: I've heard enough. It was Drum.

nodes.text: Jun Solis. Independent Frontier trader. Patel flagged a discrepancy in her cargo declaration the week prior. If it stuck, she'd lose docking privileges at Nexus Prime. For a Frontier independent, that's a slow death sentence. *Mossa frowns.* Solis badged into the cargo inspection booth to file an appeal. Says Patel wasn't at his desk so she left. But her ship's departure log shows she didn't undock until twenty minutes after the estimated seal failure. The inspection booth's terminal can read Bay C's environmental status but can't write to it. Read-only access.

responses.text: I've heard enough. It was Solis.

nodes.text: *Mossa leans back and crosses her arms.* You looked at all three. Good. Here's what I keep coming back to. Drum's loader GPS clears him unless he can teleport. Solis's booth terminal is read-only for Bay C. That leaves Vey. He was on the one terminal with write access to those pressure seals, and Patel's audit would have buried him. *She shrugs.* But I'm dock security, not a detective. That's why you're here. Your call.

responses.text: Give me a minute with this.

nodes.text: This goes on the record. Station security acts on whatever name I file. No take-backs. Who killed Reshad Patel?

responses.text: Torrin Vey. He had the access and the motive.
Kess Drum. The grudge was personal.
I need to think about this.
File all three names. Let the prosecutor sort the access logs. Whoever it was, the other two know something they're not sharing.

nodes.text: *Mossa types the name and hits confirm without hesitation.* Vey. The supervisor's terminal, the audit motive, and a career's worth of reasons to keep Patel quiet. Commerce Guild will send lawyers before he's even in a holding cell, but the access logs don't lie. *She slides a credit chit across the desk.* Consultation fee. Officially you were never here.
